It is a great comfort to all believers that whoever opposes them, God opposes; and they may approach him as one who is pleased to care for them. David's enemies mocked him for his devotion, but they could not make him abandon it.

The Lord Jesus may speak here as a Judge, condemning some of his enemies to warn others. When people reject the salvation of Christ, even their prayers are counted among their sins. See what drives some to shameful deaths and ruins the families and estates of others, making them and theirs despicable and hateful, and bringing poverty, shame, and misery upon their descendants: it is sin, that mischievous, destructive thing. And what will be the effect of the sentence, “Go, you cursed,” on the bodies and souls of the wicked! How it will affect the senses of the body and the powers of the soul with pain, anguish, horror, and despair! Think on these things, sinners, tremble and repent.

The psalmist takes God’s comforts to himself in a very humble way. He was troubled in mind. His body was wasted and almost worn away. But it is better to be thin in body while the soul prospers and is healthy than to be thin in soul while the body is well-fed. He was ridiculed and reproached by his enemies. But if God blesses us, we need not care who curses us; for how can they curse whom God has not cursed, but rather whom he has blessed? He pleads for God’s glory and the honor of his name. Save me, not according to my merit, because I claim none, but according to your mercy. He concludes with the joy of faith, in assurance that his present conflicts would end in triumphs. Let all who suffer according to the will of God commit the keeping of their souls to him. Jesus, unjustly put to death and now risen again, is an Advocate and Intercessor for his people, ever ready to appear on their behalf against a corrupt world and the great accuser.
